It’s common to look for clear connections to how these changes influence modeling, governance, integration, and consumption of data in practice. With SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise strategy and the announcement of the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) at Sapphire 2026, many data professionals are asking whether this represents a real architectural shift or simply a reframing of existing capabilities.

This post is written for practitioners already familiar with SAP Datasphere, SAP BW, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud, and SAP Master Data Governance, and focuses on what this strategy means in technical and operational terms for data professionals working with BDC today. It also offers a completely new perspective to data professionals who have never worked with these technologies and are intrigued by SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise vision.

What is not changing

Before discussing what’s new, it’s important to establish what remains true.

The Autonomous Enterprise doesn’t imply unattended automation or a loss of human supervision. AI agents operate within boundaries defined by data models, semantics, authorizations, and governance policies. Human intent, stewardship, and architectural decisions remain foundational. BDC is also not being replaced. BAIP doesn’t sit alongside BDC as a competing data platform. It builds on BDC and depends on it. Without harmonized data, preserved business context, and governed semantics, the Autonomous Enterprise cannot function reliably.

If anything, this strategy reinforces that strong data foundations are not optional for AI, they are prerequisites.

Why SAP is changing the AI engagement model

Most enterprise AI to date has been embedded inside applications. Predictive scores, recommendations, or copilots are scoped to a single process or system, often with limited visibility across business domains. The Autonomous Enterprise introduces a different model: AI on applications. Instead of embedding AI inside each application, SAP is introducing an agent‑based engagement layer that operates across applications, workflows, and data domains. These agents can reason over business intent, traverse processes, and execute actions using governed enterprise data. This shift only works if AI can reliably understand business meaning at scale. That requirement is what makes BAIP and BDC inseparable.

BAIP from a data architecture perspective

From a technical standpoint, SAP Business AI Platform is not a single service or runtime. It is a composition of cloud services that bring together AI models, agents, orchestration, governance, and data access into a coherent platform. Within BAIP, BDC makes the core of “Contextualize and Reason” services along with SAP Knowledge Graph. Its role is to provide AI agents with access to data that already carries business semantics, lineage, and policy enforcement. This includes SAP‑managed data products, customer‑defined derived data products, and harmonized data across SAP and non‑SAP sources using zero‑copy and federated data access patterns.

With a deep business process expertise that no one can match, SAP systems contain the most critical business data, forming the center of gravity for data and AI applications. Just to illustrate the size of SAP’s metadata model, SAP Knowledge Graph for SAP S/4HANA alone is based on 452,000 ABAP tables, 80,000 CDS views, and 7.3 million fields. That said, AI agents do not reason over raw tables or loosely defined datasets. They reason over data products with defined meaning, relationships, and access rules. BDC is the system that supplies those constructs.

 SAP Business AI Platform

How this addresses real data practitioner challenges

For data practitioners, the relevance of the Autonomous Enterprise strategy becomes clear when mapped to long‑standing pain points. 

Data context loss across systems remains one of the most expensive problems in enterprise data landscapes. Even modern architectures often strip business meaning as data moves between systems. In the Autonomous Enterprise model, AI agents rely on BDC’s business data fabric to preserve object relationships, hierarchies, and semantics across SAP and non‑SAP environments.

Semantic inconsistency is another persistent issue. KPIs and dimensions drift as data is replicated and remodeled. Under BAIP, semantic definitions authored in BDC – through Datasphere models, BW objects, HANA calculation views, and governed master data – are reused not only for analytics, but also for AI‑driven reasoning and action.

Manual orchestration and governance overhead is the third major friction point. In the Autonomous Enterprise architecture, governance is enforced by design. AI agents discover and consume data through BDC services that already apply authorization policies, data product contracts, and stewardship rules. Governance becomes intrinsic to execution rather than an after‑the‑fact control.

How this maps to BDC components

At a practical level, the Autonomous Enterprise strategy doesn’t introduce a new data stack. It activates existing BDC components in a broader execution context.

  • SAP Datasphere continues to provide semantic modeling, data product definition, and cross‑system harmonization. Those models are no longer consumed only by dashboards and planners, but also by AI agents operating through BAIP.
  • SAP BW remains a governed source of enterprise semantics and historical business logic, with its models surfaced as data products that retain their meaning when consumed by analytics, planning, or AI.
  • SAP HANA Cloud contributes intelligent compute, advanced SQL, and increasingly AI‑aware capabilities that support both transactional and analytical data products used by agents and applications.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud remains the primary analytical and planning consumption layer, while also becoming a beneficiary of AI‑assisted insight generation grounded in the same governed data products.
  • SAP Master Data Governance provides the quality and consistency layer that ensures AI agents reason over accurate, harmonized master data rather than fragmented representations.

Together, these components form the trusted knowledge core that BAIP relies on to make agent‑based AI reliable and explainable.

The technical takeaway

The Autonomous Enterprise strategy doesn’t reduce the importance of data engineering, modeling, or governance. It raises the bar. Putting AI on applications only works when AI can rely on consistent semantics, preserved business context, and enforced governance. BDC provides that foundation. BAIP operationalizes it. For data professionals, this strategy is less about adopting a new platform and more about extending existing BDC practices into an AI‑first execution model.

Join the conversation

These changes will surface new architectural patterns, design trade‑offs, and best practices. The Autonomous Enterprise will not be built in a single release. It will be built incrementally on the data foundations practitioners are already creating today. Join the Data Professionals community discussions to exchange technical perspectives, share lessons learned, and explore how others are evolving their BDC foundations to support agent‑based AI.

entry.title: The AI Isn't the Problem. The Data Underneath It Is.
entry.content:
Most AI projects stall in the same place. The pilot worked. The model performed. Leadership approved the next phase. And then, without a clean explanation, the path to production got complicated.

It's rarely the AI. It's almost always the data.

Two-thirds of business leaders say they don't fully trust their data. For organizations trying to move AI from experiment to enterprise capability, that's not a survey result - it's a structural problem. The gap between a convincing proof of concept and a scalable, reliable AI deployment is, in most cases, a data readiness gap.

This month, that gap gets a framework.

---

## What's coming

The Open Data Institute (ODI), SAP's independent research partner on the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI (IDEA), is releasing A Framework for AI-Ready Enterprise Data in late June. It's a rigorous, vendor-agnostic treatment of the problem: built from a comparative analysis of 23 published frameworks from organizations including Gartner, McKinsey, EY, Accenture, and Google Cloud, validated through expert interviews with senior data leaders at NTT Data, Clorox, and ZEISS, and grounded in 14 years of ODI experience building independent, trustworthy data infrastructure.

The result is 21 concrete, actionable criteria across four pillars - dataset properties, metadata, surrounding infrastructure, and governance - each calibrated for the enterprise environment and the specific demands of agentic AI.

It's not a whitepaper with broad recommendations. It's a practical checklist.

---

## The shape of the problem

The framework defines AI-ready data as "data that is technically optimized for machine learning, high quality and adherent to standards, legally compliant, and responsibly collected."

What makes the enterprise version significantly harder is scale, interconnection, and the nature of how AI now interacts with data.

Enterprise data doesn't live in tidy, isolated repositories. A customer record exists in the CRM, billing, and logistics systems - each version slightly different, each updated independently. A product code means one thing in finance and something adjacent in supply chain. That fragmentation is manageable when humans navigate it; people apply judgment, know which system to trust for which question. AI agents don't do that. They use what they can access, and they use it with confidence.

The problem compounds with agentic AI - systems that don't just answer queries but independently search for data, write and run code to analyze it, take actions based on what they find, and generate new data as a byproduct. That interaction pattern introduces failure modes that traditional data pipelines don't surface. A schema changes silently. A data feed shifted. The agent keeps running, producing outputs that look authoritative but are built on a corrupted foundation. The ODI framework calls this a "data cascade" - and it's the failure mode that scales with your AI adoption, not against it.

For anyone following SAP's Autonomous Enterprise strategy and the Business AI Platform announced at SAPPHIRE 2026, this should sound familiar. The premise of AI agents reasoning across SAP and non-SAP systems - traversing business processes, executing actions, generating outputs - depends entirely on the data foundation underneath being consistent, governed, and trustworthy. BAIP operationalizes the vision. The ODI framework is an independent measure of whether your data is ready for it.

The framework's 21 criteria address this directly. They cover how datasets should be structured and documented, how master records should be governed to establish a single source of truth, how metadata needs to evolve from descriptive labels into a machine-readable semantic map of the business, and how access controls need to be enforced at the infrastructure level rather than through natural language policies that agents can misinterpret or users can bypass.

Across four pillars, the framework adds six criteria specific to enterprise - including master data management, semantic interlinkage and active cataloguing, monitoring and observability checkpoints, and agent-aware access controls via policy-as-code. These are not theoretical additions. They are the capabilities that separate organizations successfully scaling AI from those stuck cycling through pilots.

The full report is worth reading before it's public. It's a concrete, independently validated treatment - and it maps directly to the decisions experts building on SAP infrastructure are making right now.

If you've been through an AI pilot - what was the first thing that broke when you tried to scale it? The data, the governance, or something else?

---

## Get the report before it's public

The ODI report publishes at the end of June. IDEA membership is free - and includes early access to the report along with a peer community of senior data leaders working through exactly these questions.

Join IDEA and request early access to the report

---

IDEA - the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI - is a programme of independent research and peer learning developed in partnership between SAP and the Open Data Institute. Nine roundtables. 140+ senior executives. Three research briefs. And a framework that's about to change how enterprise AI readiness gets measured.

entry.title: Why Business Meaning Matters in Modern Analytics Landscapes
entry.content:
From the introduction to Part I

In the introduction, we saw how organizations now operate analytics across multiple platform teams - SAP data platform teams, cloud data platform teams, and BI reporting teams. This opening introduces three key problems: metrics drift, duplicated logic, and AI amplifying chaos.

This post explores the first and most fundamental problem: why business meaning fragments in the first place.

How business meaning fragments as organizations scale

A familiar pattern in organizations with multiple platforms looks something like this:

  • One report becomes a reporting landscape
  • One BW Query becomes several variants
  • One platform becomes two or three
  • And local optimization starts to accumulate

At that point, the issue is rarely whether the platforms work. Usually, they do. The issue is whether the same number still means the same thing everywhere it appears.

That is the moment where scale starts to work against consistency.

When business meaning is not defined explicitly, it tends to fragment across many different places:

 

That often leads to well-known symptoms:

  • The same KPI shows different values depending on which tool you use (e.g., Finance reports €10M revenue while Sales reports €10.2M for the same period)
  • Finance or controlling has trouble reconciling reports
  • Small changes become expensive because logic is duplicated

None of this is new. These issues exist in any analytics environment. Multi-platform landscapes amplify them significantly - the same business logic must be implemented multiple times across different data platforms, each with different development paradigms and SQL semantics.

That is where Business Data Architecture becomes useful: it provides a more explicit way to organize meaning, platform responsibility, and reuse.

 

How Business Data Architecture works

Business Data Architecture is an architectural operating model for modern analytics environments - not a new platform, but a way to organize meaning and data across the platforms you already have.

It works by making four things explicit:

  • Definition: Business meaning is defined once, independently of any platform
  • Platform Responsibility: Clear boundaries for which platform owns which business domains
  • Transformation: Change happens in controlled layers with explicit constraints
  • Reuse: Data crosses platform boundaries without silent redefinition

That matters because as analytics scales, the number of teams, implementations, and decisions that need to stay aligned grows faster than informal coordination can handle.

  • Once analytics spans multiple platforms, preserving meaning becomes just as important as moving data.

 

Why this is also relevant for AI-assisted delivery

One reason this architecture matters is that it does not treat AI as the starting point. Instead, it treats explicit architecture as the prerequisite.

If business meaning, platform responsibility, and execution rules are unclear, AI will not fix that. It will usually scale the inconsistency faster.

 

  • If those elements are explicit, AI can support design and build in a more controlled way
  • If they are implicit, automation mainly increases risk

AI development tools like Joule for Developers, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, and agentic platforms amplify what's already there. Give them fragmented business logic, and they'll help you fragment it faster across more platforms. The architecture presented in this series positions organizations to use AI assistance more safely - explicit patterns and validation rules constrain what AI can generate, reducing the error rates that plague unconstrained AI code generation.

That is why this series starts with meaning and structure before it gets anywhere near AI-operable delivery.

 

What the rest of the series builds on

The rest of the posts are built on this starting point:

  • First, define business meaning clearly enough to reuse it
  • Then, establish clear platform responsibility across your landscape
  • Then, structure execution so it remains consistent
  • Only after that does AI-operable delivery become trustworthy at scale

That progression is what makes the architecture useful in practice, especially in landscapes where SAP and non-SAP platforms need to work together.

 

Up next

The next post starts with the foundation:

Defining business meaning before choosing the platform

  • Why metrics, measures, and dimensions need to stay stable across tools and platforms
  • And why that question comes before platform design

entry.title: Business Data Architecture for Modern Analytics Landscapes
entry.content:
Defining meaning, preventing drift, building trust

If you work with analytics today, your organization faces a familiar challenge: business meaning fragments as analytics scales.

It shows up in different ways: metrics calculated differently across teams, business logic duplicated in data models and reports, the same term meaning different things to different stakeholders. As analytics grows - more teams, more tools, more use cases - implicit understanding breaks down.

Multi-platform environments (SAP alongside Snowflake, Databricks, or Fabric) amplify this challenge significantly, but the root problem exists regardless of platform count.

In this blog series, I introduce Business Data Architecture as a practical way to address that challenge. The goal is not to promote a single platform or a big-bang transformation. It is an architectural operating model for modern analytics environments - a way to organize meaning and data across the platforms you already have.

The Challenge in Modern Analytics Landscapes

 

These challenges emerge as analytics scales - whether within a single platform or across multiple platforms.

Multi-platform landscapes amplify them significantly because the same business logic must be implemented multiple times across different data platforms (SAP Datasphere, Snowflake, Databricks), each with different development paradigms and SQL semantics. Coordination costs increase, drift is easier to introduce, and duplication becomes structural rather than accidental.

The architectural principles apply regardless of platform count - single-platform organizations gain governance and consistency, while multi-platform organizations additionally gain cross-platform coordination.

 

 What this series explores

 The starting point is simple:

  • If business meaning is not defined explicitly, it becomes harder to preserve as analytics scales across platforms.

Across the series, I show how Business Data Architecture is designed to:

  • Define business meaning through metrics, measures, dimensions, and datasets
  • Make platform responsibility explicit without centralizing everything
  • Use modeling layers and data products to structure reuse
  • Separate business intent from technical implementation
  • Create the architectural conditions for AI-operable delivery without losing control

So, this is not really a series about technology choice. It is a series about how to make heterogeneous analytics landscapes work more coherently.

 

Built on proven architectural foundations

This architecture extends and combines established data and domain modeling approaches. It does not replace them - it brings them together into a consistent, governed, and AI-operable delivery model.

Four Proven Foundations, One Unified Architecture

 

How the series is structured

The posts are organized into three parts, each building progressively from business meaning to AI-operable delivery:

From Business Meaning to AI-Operable Delivery

 

The complete series:

  • Introduction: Business Data Architecture for Modern Analytics Landscapes

Part I: Define Business Meaning

  • Post 1: Why Business Meaning Matters
  • Post 2: Defining Business Meaning

Part II: Organize for Coherence

  • Post 3: Platform Responsibility
  • Post 4: Modeling Layers
  • Post 5: Data Products

Part III: Execute with Control

  • Post 6: Transformations - Defining How Data Changes Consistently
  • Post 7: Artifacts - Standardized Building Blocks with Role-Based Constraints
  • Post 8: Data Product Patterns - Composition Blueprints that Become Executable Skills
  • Post 9: AI-Operable Delivery - Executing Pattern Skills with Boundaries
  • Post 10: Closing Reflection - From Architecture to Action

The idea is to move step by step from business meaning and ownership to execution, repeatability, and AI-operable delivery.

What this series is not

This series is not intended as:

  • A tool comparison
  • A how-to guide for a specific platform
  • A centralization strategy

It is closer to an architectural lens for thinking about analytics in open, multi-platform environments.

  • It assumes platform diversity will remain
  • It makes ownership and reuse more explicit
  • And it aims to improve consistency without reducing local autonomy

 

Getting started

If these challenges sound familiar, the first post starts with the core issue behind many analytics problems: 

  • business meaning becomes fragmented long before anyone notices it.

From there, the series builds toward a more structured and scalable delivery model. 

Read about Why Business Meaning Matters in Modern Analytics Landscapes here

entry.title: Working with BDC in the SAP Business AI Platform: The Data Analyst Perspective
entry.content:
As a data analyst working with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), you already rely on governed data models, consistent KPIs, and trusted semantics to produce analytics and insights. The Autonomous Enterprise strategy with the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) doesn’t change that foundation, it extends it.

What does change is how insights are generated, explained, and acted on. Analytics is no longer the final step in the data value chain. It becomes an active input into AI‑driven reasoning and execution, grounded in the same business context that you define and validate today. At the core of this shift is how BDC participates in the Contextualize and Reason services of BAIP, alongside the SAP Knowledge Graph. BDC provides the harmonized data foundation and business semantics, while the SAP Knowledge Graph encodes relationships and business context. Together, they form the trusted context layer that AI agents rely on to reason and act.

What stays the same

Your core analytical responsibilities remain intact. You continue to work with semantic models defined in SAP Datasphere, SAP BW, and SAP HANA Cloud, accessing SAP and non-SAP data, where business meaning is explicitly modeled. These models, together with master data governed through SAP Master Data Governance, form the structured input to the knowledge graph that AI relies on. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) remains your primary environment for analysis, visualization, and planning.

The difference is that these analytical assets are no longer only consumed by humans, they are part of the contextual foundation used by AI agents.

What changes in practice

The most important shift is that AI now consumes the same semantic and contextual layer you work with. In the Autonomous Enterprise, AI agents do not generate insights from isolated datasets. They reason over BDC data products enriched with relationships captured in the SAP Knowledge Graph. When an AI agent produces a narrative, explains a deviation, or recommends an action, it operates on top of business objects, hierarchies, and dependencies that you already know.

This changes the validation process. Instead of questioning the origin of an AI insight, you can trace it back to governed data products and their relationships within the broader business context. Analytics shifts from being a downstream consumer of data to being a shared semantic contract between humans and AI.

How AI‑assisted analytics works with BDC

AI‑assisted insight generation doesn’t bypass your analytical models. It builds on them. Data products defined in BDC, whether sourced from SAP applications, BW objects, or Datasphere models, carry business meaning, lineage, and access rules. BAIP uses these data products as the context layer for AI reasoning. When AI generates narratives, summaries, or explanations, it does so against this governed semantic layer.

For you as a data analyst, this means faster exploration and explanation without sacrificing consistency. You spend less time reconciling definitions and more time interpreting results.

Impact on trust and validation

One of the biggest challenges with AI‑generated insights is trust. With BAIP, trust is achieved by design. Because AI agents rely on the same BDC‑managed semantics you use, AI outputs inherit the same KPI logic, hierarchies, and filters. Access controls and data policies are enforced consistently, whether the consumer is a dashboard, a planner, or an AI agent.

This dramatically reduces the need for parallel validation loops and manual reconciliation.

How this maps to BDC components

For data analysts, the component mapping is straightforward.

  • SAP Datasphere continues to be the primary place where analytical semantics are modeled and exposed as data products.
  • SAP BW continues to provide enterprise‑grade, historically rich business logic that remains authoritative for many KPIs.
  • SAP HANA Cloud supports advanced analytical queries and high‑performance access to data products.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud remains the main consumption layer for analysis, planning, and increasingly AI‑assisted insight generation.
  • SAP Master Data Governance ensures that master data consistency underpins all analytical and AI‑driven outcomes.

Together, these components form the trusted analytical foundation that AI agents consume through BAIP.

What to focus on now

As a data analyst, the most valuable preparation is not learning new AI tools. It is strengthening the analytical foundation AI will rely on. Focus on well‑defined KPIs, stable semantics, clear lineage, and reusable data products. The better your analytical models are today, the more reliable AI‑assisted insights will be tomorrow.

The takeaway

BAIP doesn’t replace analytics with AI. It connects analytics and AI through a shared semantic foundation. For data analysts, this means higher leverage: the models you build and validate do not just inform people, they inform AI‑driven decisions as well.

Join the Data Professionals community discussions to share analytical patterns, discuss AI‑assisted insights, and explore how others are evolving their BDC analytics foundations in the Autonomous Enterprise era.

This post is a follow up to the previous post From ‘AI in Applications’ to ‘AI on Applications’. It is the first in a series that explores how the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) impacts day‑to‑day work across key BDC personas, starting with the data analyst perspective.

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with BAS, Python, Claude and Cline
entry.content:
Have a Large Language Model write the Machine Learning code for you next project!

Sit back and watch the SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) use Anthropic Claude to create Jupyter Notebooks and write the Python code to trigger the Predictive Analysis Library in SAP HANA Cloud (or SAP Datasphere). This example uses Claude, but there is a long list of models to choose from through SAP AI Core. In this tutorial we go through a time-series forecast, but that's just an example, you can use this ability to vibe code in BAS for all sorts of requirements.

It's fantastic to see how the code is written automatically, and how good the code is! But to keep it real, work with that automatically created code as if it had been written by a human intern. Inspect the code to ensure it does what you want it to do.

  • Architecture
  • Prerequisites
  • Setup
  • Vibe coding test
  • Adding the latest documentation
  • Vibe code HANA Machine Learning
  • Summary

Big thanks go to @sherwin_emami for introducing me to Cline and for showing me the ropes of vibe coding in BAS!

 

Architecture

The architecture centers around the SAP Business Application Studio, where you can already do you Python scripting in a Jupyter Notebook by hand. 

The coding agent Cline is installed as extension in BAS. Cline connects to SAP AI Core to integrate the Large Language Models (LLM) that process the user requests and produce the code. You can choose which LLM you want to use. In this example we use Claude, which is hosted outside the SAP environment. Other models, i.e. from Mistral, are hosted on SAP infrastructure.

SAP HANA Cloud holds the data and Machine Learning algorithms. The same setup would also work with the SAP HANA Cloud that is embedded in SAP Datasphere.

Optionally, you can also add the Context7 MCP server to Cline to get access to additional / current software documentation.

You may have heard of the SAP Business AI Platform, which is also shown in the architecture diagram. However, here we use the same components that you know from the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). You can implement this scenario on any BTP environment.

 

Prerequisites

You will need the following components to implement this blog's scenario:

  • SAP Business Application Studio, configured to run Jupyter Notebooks, see this blog
  • SAP HANA Cloud
    • configured to run the Predictive Analysis Library, see the same blog
    • with the table HOTELNIGHTS from this other blog uploaded
  • SAP AI Core, on an Extended Service Plan. SAP AI Launchpad is not needed for the vibe coding.

 

Setup

Install the Cline extension into the BAS. This should take only a few seconds.

 

Then get a Service Key from your SAP AI Core instance.

 

Continue in the Cline extension and give it access to SAP AI Core.

  • Select the option "Bring my own API key".
  • From the "API Provider" dropdown select "SAP AI Core" and enter the values from your SAP AI Core Service Key:
    • AI Core Client ID: value of clientid
    • AI Core Client Secret: value of clientsecret
    • AI Core Base URL: value of AI_API_URL
    • AI Core Auth URL: value of url
    • AI Core Resource Group: By default the Resource Group is "default", unless you created your own in SAP AI Core
  • Tick "Orchestration Mode".  This makes all applicable models from SAP AI Core available, without having to deploy them first
  • Select your preferred model. Here I am going with Claude 4.7 Opus.

 

Click "Continue" and Cline is asking what it can do your you 

 

Vibe coding test

Use a simple task to get familiar with Cline. It's important to know the distinction between "Plan" and "Act" mode. 

Plan mode: Typically it is best to start in Plan mode. You describe what you would Cline built for you and Cline is building a plan for achieving this. This can be a discussion, which shapes how to address this task. In this phase, Cline can access files (if you grant access), but it cannot make any changes to your existing file or create new files.

Act mode: Once you are happy with the plan you can switch to the "Act" mode, in which Cline executes the plan. Here Cline can, for instance, create new files or modify existing ones.

Let's try it out!

First select the folder in which you want to work, ie "home/user/projects/hanaml". If you are unsure about the folder, see this blog.

Make sure that Cline is in "Plan" mode (the little button below the prompt window). Check which permissions you want to give Cline. Then enter this task and Cline is creating the plan to achieve this.

Create a jupyter notebook that contains a greeting

 

We are happy with the plan and want this executed. Click on the "Act" tab. You will see how Cline is going through the plan. If you are using the default permissions, you will be asked to confirm the creation of the Notebook file and indeed the file opens up!

 

You can run the code in the notebook. And the file is there as requested. 

 

Mission accomplished, the vibe coding test was successful!

 

A few hints in case this didn't work for you:

  • With Cline's default settings you will probably get a few approval requests. You need to confirm these for the process to continue.
  • These requests are not always immediately shown on screen. Check whether you need to scroll further down in the Cline window to see the latest output.
  • Sometimes Cline seems to hang. Refreshing the browser seems to fix it for me
  • Sometimes Cline opening a file results in the error "The editor could not be opened due to an unexpected error. Please consult the log for more details.". Clicking the "Try again" usually fixes it for me.
  • You know that the "Act" mode has completed, when you see "Start New Task".

 

Adding the latest documentation

Before creating code for your business, let's make Cline even more useful. Currently the Large Language Model that we are using might not know the latest documentation of the libraries that we want to use, particularly the Python package hana_ml.

A popular approach for bringing in the latest documentation is by adding Context7 to the project. There may be alternatives, but Context7 worked well for me. It can be added as MCP server both as local installation and through a remote URL. The local installation through the Cline interface didn't succeed for me, but the remote version worked very well. Funnily enough, you can just ask Cline to add the remote version itself.

Go into the "Plan" mode and request:

Add an MCP server for Context7, use the remote URL

When happy with the plan, switch to the "Act" mode. Confirm the requests if you agree. And the MCP server for Context7 should have been added.

 

Verify this in the Cline settings. In the "MCP Servers" section under "Configure" the new MCP Server should show up. Also, below the prompt window you can see that the new MCP server is enabled and ready for use.

 

Vibe code HANA Machine Learning

Everything we need has been set up. Let's do something useful with this it. This blog has a step-by-step explanation for writing the code to carry out a time-series forecast in SAP HANA Cloud. Hopefully that tutorial is useful to learn how the Machine Learning works, how Python code can instruct the Predictive Analysis Library to create the forecasts, without having to extract the data. Let's put vibe coding to the test, whether it can create such code for us.

First ensure that you have the credentials.json file in your folder, as shown in the same blog.

Then ask your code assistant in "Plan" mode to create the code.

Create a new notebook called “HANA ML Time-series forecast”.  Use the details from @/credentials.json to and the hana_ml package to connect to SAP HANA Cloud. Connect to the table HOTELNIGHTS. Do not download the data. Use hana_ml to have HANA Cloud aggregate this table on the MONTH column, summarising the column HOTELNIGHTS. Use the hana_ml Package to train an AdditiveModelForecast model and predict the next 6 months. Join the actuals and the prediction in a single structure and save as table HOTELNIGHTS_FORECAST_BAS. Test the code and fix any errors. Use context7.

 

It is coming back with a very detailed, pretty looking plan.

 

Click on "Act" to have it executed. Watch how Cline is going through that plan: writing, testing and potentially fixing the code. Pay attention to any Request prompts that might come up at the bottom left. Approve these if you agree. After a few minutes you should have a Notebook!

 

In the new Notebook, first choose your Python kernel on the top right. Then hit "Run All" to test the logic. It's looking good! But not perfect... The dates for the future months, that are predicted, are not the first day of that month.

 

Back to the prompt , and request a change. Here I am skipping the "Plan" mode and request this directly in the "Act" mode.

Change the code so that the forecasted dates are always the first day of the month.

 

And indeed, now the months are correctly dated! The LLMs came up with many different way to create the DataFrame with the dates that need to be predicted. You can see a few in the comments.

 

As requested, the prediction is also saved to SAP HANA Cloud.

 

Summary

It's impressive how efficient and helpful vibe coding can be! 

It can be a massive time-saver, but do remember to verify the code...

entry.title: Your AI Agent Has Three Different Definitions of "Customer." That's a Problem.
entry.content:
Source: IDEA Forum, NYC 2025

My last post made one argument: most AI projects don't stall because of the model. They stall because of the data underneath it. This post is about the most common culprit - the one that tends to surprise people, because they thought they'd already solved it.

---

## Three records. One customer. Zero clarity.

Here's the scenario.

You deploy an internal AI copilot connected to your CRM, billing system, and logistics platform. A sales manager asks it to pull together everything the company knows about a key account before a renewal meeting. The agent returns an answer - confident, formatted, comprehensive.

And it's wrong. Not because the AI failed. Because the customer has three different records across three different systems, each updated independently, none of them authoritative.

This isn't a data quality story. It's a structural one. Most large organizations carry fragmented or duplicated records for every core business entity - customers, vendors, products, employees. Humans have always navigated that fragmentation with judgment: they know which system to trust for which question. That institutional knowledge is invisible, undocumented, and entirely absent from the AI.

The agent doesn't apply judgment. It uses whatever it can access, and it uses it with confidence.

---

## What the ODI framework calls this

The ODI's Framework for AI-Ready Enterprise Data treats this as a baseline condition of enterprise data, not an edge case. It names Master Data Management, establishing a single authoritative "golden record" for each core entity, as one of six enterprise-specific criteria that separate organizations ready to scale AI from those that aren't.

The framing matters. MDM isn't new. Most SAP shops have some version of it in place. What the framework adds is a recast: MDM is no longer a data governance discipline. In an agentic world, it's AI infrastructure.

Without it, every AI system querying across your data is operating on an unverified, inconsistent foundation. The errors that produces are systematically difficult to trace. You won't see them at first. You'll see them six months in, when someone starts pulling on a thread and finds the model was confident about the wrong version of reality.

---

## What MDM at scale actually requires

The framework is specific about what this takes operationally. Designating records as authoritative isn't enough. You need entity resolution pipelines — continuous processes that actively scan across disparate systems, identify duplicates, and merge them into validated, governed golden records. Not a one-time clean-up. An ongoing operational capability.

That distinction matters. Organizations that treat MDM as a project to complete are the same ones rebuilding it two years into an AI initiative, under pressure, with a more complicated data landscape than they started with.

---

## Where SAP Business Data Cloud sits in this

This is the design premise of SAP Business Data Cloud: harmonized, business-ready data objects that unify SAP application data, S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, and the broader SAP ecosystem, into a governed, consistent layer that AI systems can trust.

The logic is direct. AI performs reliably only on data it can verify. You can't verify data without a single authoritative source. Business Data Cloud is the mechanism for establishing that source - not as a static warehouse, but as a live, governed business data layer that persists as AI workloads run against it.

For anyone evaluating or implementing Business Data Cloud: the ODI framework gives that investment independent validation. Harmonizing business objects, resolving duplicates, establishing governed master records, that's not data hygiene. It's the prerequisite for every AI use case you're planning to build on top of it.

---

## The compounding problem

There's a specific risk the framework names worth sitting with. AI systems don't just consume data, they generate it. Classifications, summaries, derived records, confidence scores. As deployments scale, AI-generated data accumulates alongside source data. Without MDM, that generated data starts to obscure original truth, and the further you get from that moment, the harder it becomes to identify which record is authoritative.

The framework calls this the "single source of truth" problem compounding under AI load. The organizations that address it before scaling will have a significantly easier time than those that retrofit it afterward.

How many authoritative versions of "customer" does your organization actually have right now? Most people I ask pause before answering.

---

## Two ways to go deeper

The ODI framework publishes at the end of June. IDEA membership is free - it includes early access to the full report and a peer community of senior data leaders working through exactly these questions.

[Join IDEA]

On June 25th, the ODI and SAP are hosting a live webinar, Building the Foundation: AI-Ready Data for the Modern Enterprise,  working through the framework directly with the team that built it.

[Register for the June 25th webinar]

---

IDEA (the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI) is a programme of independent research and peer learning developed in partnership between SAP and the Open Data Institute. Nine roundtables. 140+ senior executives. Three research briefs. And a framework that's about to change how enterprise AI readiness gets measured.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud Seamless Planning入門 - Part1 : Seamless Planningとは
entry.content:
このブログシリーズは、SAP Business Data CloudのSeamless Planningの全体像を一通り把握していただくことを目的としています。

 
このシリーズは、次の以下で構成されます。
Part1 : Seamless Planningとは（本ブログ）
Part2 :  Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう
Part3 : Seamless PlanningでSAP Datasphereの実績ビューをライブバージョンで参照しよう
to be continued ...
 
本ブログ「Part1 : Seamless Planningとは」では、Seamless Planningで「何ができるのか」「どんなアーキテクチャなのか」「どんな場面で使われるのか」を解説します。
 

目次

  1. Seamless Planningとは
  2. Seamless Planningのメリット
  3. 主なユースケース
  4. アーキテクチャの全体像
  5. 利用開始の前提条件
     

1. Seamless Planningとは

Seamless Planningは、計画・分析・データ管理を一つの環境で実現する、SAPが提供するプランニングの仕組みです。計画の入力・計算・分析はSAP Analytics Cloudで行い、そのデータはデータ基盤のSAP Datasphereに直接格納されます。データの転送や同期なしに分析・予測・AI活用につなげることができます。

2. Seamless Planningのメリット

  • 計画ツールとデータウェアハウスが一体
    SAP Analytics Cloudで作成した計画データはSAP Datasphereに直接格納されるため、計画値の更新がそのままデータウェアハウスに反映されます。データのエクスポートや手動連携は不要です。計画担当者が数値を入力・公開すると、SAP Datasphere側のビューやレポートにも即時に反映されます。

  • 実績データとのリアルタイム比較が可能
    SAP S/4HANAなどの基幹システムからSAP Datasphereに取り込まれた実績データを、計画モデル内でリアルタイムに参照できます。「計画 対 実績」の差異分析を、データの複製なしに常に最新の状態で行えます。

  • AI/MLとの統合が容易
    SAP Business Data CloudではDatatbricksやSnowflakeなどの外部のオープンデータエコシステムとゼロコピーでデータの共有を行うことができます。計画データも同じ基盤上にあることで、時系列予測・機械学習モデルの結果を計画プロセスに組み込むことが追加のデータ複製なしに可能です。

3. 主なユースケース

Seamless Planningはさまざまな計画業務に対応しています。代表的なユースケースを紹介します。
  • 損益(P&L)計画
    売上・コスト・粗利などの財務計画をSAP Analytics Cloudで立案。SAP Datasphereに格納されたSAP S/4HANAの実績データとリアルタイムで比較しながら、予算や予測を策定します。

  • ドライバーベース計画とシナリオ分析
    売上数量・単価・為替レートなどのビジネスドライバーをもとに計算ロジックを組み、楽観・悲観・標準などの複数シナリオを並行して管理します。外部データ（物価指数・市場データ）もSAP Datasphereから直接参照して予測精度を高められます。

  • ローリング予測
    月次・四半期ごとに予測を更新するローリングフォーキャストも、SAP Datasphereの実績を直接参照することで最新の実績をすぐに取り込みながら実行できます。

  • xP&A（全社統合計画）
    財務計画だけでなく、人員計画・サプライチェーン計画・マーケティング予算など、複数部門の計画データをSAP Datasphere上で統合したり、他の計画モデルに対してライブで参照したりすることによって横断的な分析・調整を行うことができます。

 

4. アーキテクチャの全体像

ポイント：
1️⃣SAP Analytics Cloudで 定義した計画モデルのデータをSAP Datasphereに保管
2️⃣ SAP Datasphereにある実績のビューに対して計画モデルからライブバージョンとして接続
3️⃣ SAP BDC Connectで連携してきた外部データやAI予測データも同様に参照
4️⃣ 他の計画モデルの計画データに対してもライブバージョンとして接続して参照
 

5. 利用前の前提条件

Seamless Planningを利用するには、以下の条件を満たしている必要があります。
  • 同一データセンター：SAP Analytics CloudとSAP Datasphereが同じリージョンで稼働していること
  • SAP HANA Cloud基盤：SAP Analytics CloudテナントがSAP HANA Cloud上で動作していること（2024年中頃以降の新規テナントは標準対応）
  • 1対1テナント関係：SAP Analytics CloudテナントとSAP Datasphereテナントが1対1で接続されていること  
     

以上、Seamless Planningとは？を簡単に解説しました。次回のブログでは、実際にSeamless Planningモデルを作成し、Datasphere側でデータを確認する手順を解説します。


参考資料・ブログ

- シームレスな計画について | SAP Help Portal

- Seamless Planning with Datasphere & SAC: Seamless Planning in the Cloud
- Real-time steering with LIVE planning

entry.title: Live Data Connectivity in SAP Analytics Cloud - Overview and Direction
entry.content:
Over the past year and a half we have been quietly introducing and expanding a new way to connect to data in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and in this blog post I'm going to explain a little bit about why we're doing this, how it works and what the future of it looks like.

For as long as SAC has existed we have had the "Live Data Model" button in the modeler. This has been our traditional way of connecting to live data and it works via connecting to existing SAP Business Warehouse (BW) queries, S/4 HANA views, and SAP HANA calc views. This allows customers who have existing data and semantic models in their on their warehouse systems and HANA databases to reuse those existing semantic models in the context of analytics cloud. Some customers have BW queries that have been around for almost 2 decades and have used them with various client tools over the years, be they BEx Analyzer, Analysis Office, Webi, Lumira, and now SAC. That semantic content is safe and can continue to be used with each new generations of tools. This is also how the SAP Datasphere (DSP) SQL views and Analytic Models work.

Lately, we've been introducing a new way of connecting to data that's not necessarily so obvious, but has enormous impact; the live data connection. The way this works is that you start creating a normal SAC model and opt to create a new model from data; the "I need to create a model from data, so that I can craft my semantic model around my data" option. If the data source that you choose supports live data, you're given the option to import the data or to consume it live. In a nutshell we are taking advantage of the Smart Data Access (SDA) in SAP HANA, to use virtual tables as the basis of the fact table (aka data foundation) and take care of setting up the fact table type for you, behind the scenes.  This approach forms the basis for both the live data connection and  Seamless Planning. 

That’s it! There’s no magic, and the best part is you don’t have to decide at the very beginning of the workflow whether you want to create a model with imported data or with live data. Federated data is not just for data engineers anymore!

In the context of planning, we use the term "live versions." This is because planning models can have multiple versions of fact data.  In fact, you can add a live version to an existing planning model. Sometimes also colloquially we refer to the live data connection as "SQL live" even though that is a misnomer, because not all supported data sources use SQL interfaces.

Who classic Live Data Models are for and when

• Users who have existing semantic models in S/4, BW, or HANA (e.g., BW queries or HANA calc views) that they want to re-use.

SAC acts as a runtime client in this scenario and the semantic model is a black box from SAC’s perspective, so if you want to control the semantic model from the SAC side, this is not the best choice.

Who Live Data Connectivity is for and when

• Users building analytic models, when they want to ensure that the remote data source is the single source of truth.
• Planners, when they don’t want to replicate data into the actuals version, especially when that source is a large data lake.  This is also how Seamless Planning works.
• Others who don’t want the complexity of scheduling load jobs. For example, suppose you have a shared Excel file where several users are collectively editing quarterly risk analysis. If you wanted a risk analysis dashboard based on that data, you might find it very convenient if that dashboard is always based on the current values in the Excel table.

Why we're building out the live data connection

The traditional Analytics Cloud data model required you to replicate or import your data into SAC. In some respects, this is very simple. You simply connect to the data, copy it into Analytics Cloud, and then you build your semantic model on top of that for use in Analytics Cloud. If you want to update this data later, then you can always set up and schedule a load job to periodically update the data. You can bring data in from multiple sources into the same semantic model. Etcetera, etcetera.

But… We run into certain limitations in certain scenarios. For example:

• A customer may have a very large data lake. They want to do planning based on the data in this data lake, but don’t want to replicate gigabytes of data into SAC.
• Some sources are updated on a continuous basis and the replicated data might not reflect the real-time state.
• Some users may not want to be bothered with the additional effort of scheduling updates. Sure, scheduling load jobs in SAC is a far cry from data engineering, but it is still cognitive load and effort that brings minimal value in some situations.

For such situations, live data connections are a clear quality-of-life improvement.


Wait! What about DSP’s Remote Tables and BDC Zero Copy?

DSP’s Remote Tables and BDC Zero Copy are complementary to live data connections. Think of live data connections as "Federation Light." It does not require a data engineer skill set, nor does it require your SAC tenant to be in a BDC seamless planning formation. It augments DSP’s Remote Tables and BDC Zero Copy and does not replace the data engineer toolchain.

 

Which Connections are supported for live

As of mid-2026, live data connectivity is supported for:
• Google BigQuery - was the first data source enabled with this capability.
• Microsoft Azure SQL Database - added in quarterly release (QRC) 2026.Q2.
• Snowflake - added in QRC 2026.Q1.

Support for SharePoint Lists and Excel files in SharePoint are planned for QRC 2026.Q4. Other sources—especially the popular cloud data lakes—are planned to follow.

Authentication

Authentication to the remote data source is currently only supported for a service or technical user, as is the case with DSP remote tables. This means that if you need to use record level data access rules from the original source, you will have to replicate the access tables in the source into SAC data access controls (DAC). This is the same behavior that we have in DSP today. We are currently working on enabling user propagation to the remote source, which would allow you to use the remote data access tables with live connectivity in SAC without replicating your DAC. This will be a centralized BDC service for both SAC and DSP, though it will be available first in SAC. It is not on the Roadmap Explorer yet, but when we have a clearer idea of the release quarter, we will publish it.


Getting Started with Live Data Connections

To get started with live data connections, you want to ensure that your SAC tenant is on HANA Cloud and that live data connections are enabled on your tenant.

You can find the HANA version by checking the About page. You can find the About page by locating your user profile button, clicking it, and then selecting About at the bottom of the popup. The HANA version will be displayed on the About page. If your HANA version is not yet HANA Cloud, be advised that we are in the midst of migrating customer tenants over and it should be available in the course of the next few months, but you can also check with your account team to see when your tenant will be migrated.

Next, make sure that live data connections are enabled on your tenant. Doing this will enable SAC models to use live data when the data source supports it. To do this, go to the Administration module, select the Data Source Configuration tab, and ensure that Enable Data Access Agent is toggled on. This will enable the microservice that enables live data connectivity for most sources.

Now you are ready to set up the data sources in the Connections module.

Important note about connections that use the Cloud Agent or Open Connectors for import connectivity

Any source using a JDBC connection via the Cloud Agent or Open Connectors for import connectivity will behave a little bit differently between live and imported. This is because the import connection runs over the Cloud Agent via JDBC, or via BTP Open Connectors, while the live connection runs directly from SAC to the data source, cloud-to-cloud.

For example, if you have configured your Cloud Agent for import but not enabled the Enable Data Access Agent option, then you will only see the option to import data. Conversely, if you have not configured JDBC import via the Cloud Agent but have toggled Enable Data Access Agent on, then you will only have live connectivity. Also, the non-JDBC (live) connection needs its own entry in the Connections module. The tenant that I took the screenshots from does not have a Cloud Agent connected to it, but it does have live connectivity enabled and a Snowflake driver and an Azure SQL driver enabled. Therefore, on my tenant I only see the live option.

This behavior currently affects Snowflake and Microsoft Azure SQL Database connectivity. Google BigQuery is cloud-to-cloud for both import and live connectivity and is not affected. The legacy Microsoft SharePoint Excel connection runs over Open Connectors, so the upcoming Microsoft SharePoint Excel connection will have this behavior, but only temporarily. It is not in the Roadmap Explorer yet, but by mid-2027 we plan to release a new, better Microsoft SharePoint Excel connection that does not use Open Connectors. There is no legacy Microsoft SharePoint Lists connection for import, so until we can add the import option, it will be live only.

Useful Links

KBA 2928379

Configuring a Google BigQuery live connection

Configuring a Microsoft Azure SQL Database live connection

Configuring a Snowflake live connection

An excellent (external) blog post by Rafael Sachs about using Snowflake as a live source in SAC

entry.title: SAP BW Modernization and Stewardship with SAP Business Data Cloud - Introduction
entry.content:
Lift - Shift - Innovate - Govern - Decommission

This is the introduction blog of a series that covers the phases of the SAP BW modernization journey. 

For over two decades, SAP Business Warehouse (SAP BW) has served as the analytical backbone of enterprises, supporting financial reporting, operational intelligence, and regulatory compliance. Many continue to rely on SAP BW 7.5, SAP BW powered by SAP HANA, and SAP BW/4HANA to consolidate and govern mission-critical business data. 

However, the conditions under which SAP BW was originally designed have changed significantly. Enterprises now operate in a data environment characterized by larger volumes, faster decision cycles, broader integration requirements, and increased pressure to support advanced analytics and artificial intelligence. Business users no longer expect only structured historical reporting. They expect near-real-time insight, self-service access to trusted data, cross-domain semantic consistency, and support for predictive and AI-driven decision-making. At the same time, IT organizations are under constant pressure to reduce total cost of ownership, simplify operations, improve governance, and modernize aging data platforms before maintenance deadlines create forced decisions. 

These pressures are not independent. They reinforce each other. Rising infrastructure costs make it harder to justify on-premises investment. The shift to AI-ready architectures highlights the limitations of legacy modeling. The maintenance horizon places a hard upper limit on how long delay is feasible. Together, they make clear that modernization is no longer optional. The only question is how to execute it. 

This is the context in which SAP BW Modernization and Stewardship has become a strategic priority - not merely a technology upgrade, but a deliberate repositioning of the enterprise data platform for a new era. 

SAP Business Data Cloud: From Monolithic Data Warehouse to Business Data Fabric 

The architectural shift is significant. Traditional BW landscapes are centralized by design. They combine ingestion, transformation, persistence, semantic logic, and reporting in a tightly coupled model. That design brought control and consistency, but it also created bottlenecks. Over time, many enterprises found that custom logic, data duplication and silos expanded continuously, while analytical agility declined. 

SAP BDC introduces a much different architecture and philosophy. Enterprises are guided away from rigid, monolithic data warehouses toward a business data fabric and a decentralized data mesh. BW assets can be retained, exposed, modernized, governed, and eventually retired in a phased approach: Lift, Shift, Innovate, Govern, and Decommission, avoiding a high-risk big bang migration. The main phases are Lift (1), Shift (2) and Innovate (3) complemented by Govern (4) and finally Decommission (5). 

All phases form both a sequence of activities and the structure of this blog series. 

  1. Lift - Establish the foundation: Migrate SAP BW / BW4HANA on-premises to SAP BDC (SAP BW PCE) as-is to establish a stable foundation without disrupting current operations. 
  2. Shift - Begin the modernization journey: Publish SAP BW data as governed data products within SAP Business Data Cloud, making it reusable. 
  3. Innovate - Create an AI ready data platform: Adopt SAP-managed data products, intelligent applications, and cloud-native analytical patterns, replacing legacy SAP BW-centered use cases. 
  4. Govern - Ensure data integrity, compliance and user trust: Establish the stewardship controls for a sustainable data product operating model. 
  5. Decommission - Gradually retire SAP BW until it’s no longer required. 

These phases are not strictly linear. Different business domains may move through them at different speeds. Some may remain in coexistence for a while, while others modernize quickly. Governance spans the entire journey, and decommissioning should be planned early even if executed later. 

If you're interested to start the BW modernization journey follow this blog series and get your free assessment.

entry.title: Business Data Architecture: Defining Business Meaning
entry.content:
The problem: When Revenue means different things

In the previous post, we explored how business meaning fragments as organizations scale - whether that scale comes from multiple platforms, large development teams, or both.

Without clear definition, "Revenue" means different things to different teams. Finance reports €10M. Sales reports €10.2M for the same period. Both are pulling from legitimate data sources. Both calculations seem reasonable. But the numbers don't match - and nobody can explain why.

This isn't a data quality issue. It's a definition issue. The business never explicitly agreed on what "Revenue" actually means.

This post shows why this happens - and how to prevent it by separating what you measure from how you analyze it.

 

Why this happens: The report-centric pattern

This fragmentation isn't an accident. It's the predictable result of how some analytics environments evolve.

The pattern looks like this: You build a revenue report. It works. Business asks for another revenue report with different breakdowns. You copy the logic. Modify it slightly. Deploy it.

Six months later, "Revenue" means slightly different things in different places. One report includes returns, another doesn't. One deducts discounts, another shows gross. Finance's version uses invoiced amounts, Sales uses booked orders.

The business logic that defines "Revenue" is embedded in each report. There's no single place where "Revenue" is defined. Every report reimplements it.

When business requirements change - "We need to exclude cancelled orders from Revenue" - you have to find and fix every report, query, and model that calculates Revenue. And you'll miss some.

This is the report-centric approach: meaning lives in reports, reuse happens through copy/paste, and scaling means more divergence.

 

Three core concepts: Metrics, Measures, and Dimensions

Business Data Architecture prevents this by separating what you measure from how you analyze it.

In the report-centric approach, every report defines both at once. You build "Revenue vs Budget by Region" and the definition of Revenue, the Budget comparison, and the regional breakdown all live together in that one report. When someone needs a different view, they copy the logic and adjust it. That's where fragmentation starts.

Business Data Architecture breaks this apart into three explicit concepts. Each concept has a distinct responsibility, and they work together to prevent the copy/paste spiral:

If you're familiar with dimensional modeling, Measures and Dimensions aren't new - these have been standard practice since Kimball. What's different is the concept before: Metrics.

The separation between Metric and Measure is what prevents fragmentation. That separation requires the business to agree on something explicit before any analysis happens. Let's look at what that means for Revenue.

 

Metric: What the business wants to measure

"Revenue" is a metric - it represents a specific business outcome. It's defined once, independently of how you'll later analyze it.

Not "Revenue vs Budget." Not "Revenue YTD." Just "Revenue" - the monetary value from sales transactions, with explicit boundaries (what's included, what's excluded), owned by one domain (Finance).

What the business must agree on

For a metric to work as a stable foundation, the business must explicitly define three aspects:

  1. What business outcome does this represent? One clear statement. Not a list of use cases or scenarios - just what this metric fundamentally measures.
  2. What's included and what's excluded? Make the boundaries explicit. Does Revenue include returns? Are discounts already deducted? What counts and what doesn't? If you don't define this, different teams will make different assumptions.
  3. Who owns the definition? One domain - Finance, Sales, or Operations - is accountable for maintaining this metric. Not shared ownership. Not a committee. One domain owns it.

Example: How Finance defines Revenue

When Finance defines Revenue, they answer those three questions:

  1. It represents the monetary value actually generated from completed sales after deductions. That's the business outcome.
  2. It includes completed sales transactions - meaning orders that have been fulfilled and invoiced. It excludes cancelled orders. Discounts, returns, and allowances are already deducted. Those are the boundaries.
  3. Finance owns it. They're accountable for keeping this definition accurate as business processes evolve.

Notice what this definition deliberately avoids: it doesn't mention Actual vs Budget. It doesn't talk about trends or forecasts. It doesn't reference platforms or technical implementations. It just defines what Revenue fundamentally represents as a business outcome.

That's the foundation. The analytical views - Actual, Budget, Variance, Year-to-Date - come next, as measures.

 

Measure: How a metric is evaluated

A metric tells you what to measure. Measures tell you how to analyze it.

The same metric - like Revenue - can be viewed in multiple ways: Actual performance, budgeted targets, year-to-date trends, variance from plan. Each view serves a different analytical purpose.

Measures are the sanctioned analytical interpretations of a metric. They add context, comparison, and meaning - but they never redefine what the metric fundamentally represents.

Example: Revenue Measures
  • Revenue (Actual): Realized revenue from completed transactions → Result measure
  • Revenue (Budget): Planned revenue from annual budget → Performance indicator (KPI)
  • Revenue YTD: Cumulative year-to-date actual revenue → Trend analysis
  • Revenue Variance: Actual minus Budget → Explanatory context

Notice that each measure references the same "Revenue" metric. They interpret it differently - but they don't redefine it.

This separation matters because it creates stability. If "Revenue" always means the same thing - regardless of whether you're comparing it to budget, trending it over time, or breaking it down by region - then you can trust that every analysis starts from the same foundation.

 

Dimension: Business context for analysis

Dimensions provide the shared structure for analyzing metrics - Time, Organization, Product, Customer. When everyone uses the same fiscal calendar, the same organizational hierarchy, the same product categories, analyses align.

But dimensions only work when the underlying master and reference data has quality. Product categories with duplicates, organizational hierarchies with gaps, or inconsistent time periods make shared analysis impossible. Better dimension quality enables more detailed, reliable insights.

This becomes critical for self-service analytics and AI. When business users explore data or AI generates answers, they're navigating through these dimensional structures. Poor quality dimensions produce misleading analysis - regardless of whether a human or AI is doing the work.

 

Common concerns

The most immediate reaction is usually: "This sounds slower than just building reports."

It's true - the first metric takes longer when you define it properly. But the second, third, and tenth reports are faster because they reuse that definition instead of reimplementing it. More importantly: when requirements change, you fix it once - not in every report that uses it.

Another concern: "What if Finance and Sales can't agree on a common definition?"

The disagreement already exists - it's just implicit. That's why one reports €10M and the other €10.2M. Making it explicit doesn't create the problem; it surfaces it. And when genuine differences exist, separate metrics can coexist transparently: "Revenue (Finance View)" and "Revenue (Sales View)" rather than pretending they're the same thing.

Finally: "Isn't this only for greenfield implementations?"

No. You introduce it incrementally - one metric at a time. Extract the "Revenue" logic that's duplicated across five reports, define it once, and let those reports reference it. Every data platform starts with a first metric. This extends what already exists.

 

The takeaway

The report-centric approach works until it doesn't. One report becomes five. Five becomes twenty. Revenue quietly means different things in different places.

The separation changes this. You define "Revenue" once as a metric, then add measures for different analytical views. Everyone navigates using the same dimensional structure. When requirements change, you fix the definition once instead of hunting through twenty reports.

This isn't slower - it's faster at scale. The first metric takes discipline. The tenth metric proves the value.

Define what you measure. Separate how you analyze it. Build once, reuse everywhere.

 

Up next

In this post, we focused on what business meaning is and how to define it. The next post explores who owns that meaning as analytics scales:

Platform Responsibility in a Multi-Platform Analytics Landscape

  • How to make platform ownership explicit when analytics spans multiple systems
  • Ensuring metrics are defined once and shared safely - preventing duplication and drift

entry.title: The Architecture Question Your AI Strategy Can't Avoid
entry.content:
IDEA Forum - Copenhagen, 2025

The most common architectural response to an AI readiness problem is the wrong one: consolidate everything into a single system, run AI on top of it, declare the foundation solved.

It doesn't work.

Large organizations don't have a consolidation problem. They have a connection problem. A customer exists differently in three systems. A product code means one thing in finance and something adjacent in supply chain. That fragmentation is structural,  built over years of acquisitions and org changes. Centralizing it would take years and introduce a single point of failure.

The right answer isn't to move the data. It's to connect it.

---

## What the framework points to

The ODI's Framework for AI-Ready Enterprise Data,  publishing this month through the IDEA programme, defines 21 concrete criteria across four pillars. Read across them, and a pattern emerges. The requirements don't point toward centralization. They point toward connection: connecting distributed data without moving it, enriching it with semantic context, governing it in a way that holds for autonomous agents as well as humans.

That pattern has a name: a data fabric.

A data fabric is not a product, there's no software you install and declare yourself AI-ready. It's an architectural approach: integration pipelines, active catalogues, and governance engines working together to make distributed data usable. It leaves the data where it is and creates a unified virtual access layer on top - harmonizing contents at the point of query, surfacing a verified, governed response to whoever or whatever is asking.

The ODI's supplementary research on data fabrics, released alongside the main framework, maps this to three specific capabilities: integration, cataloguing, and governance.

---

## Why it matters for SAP organizations specifically

Each of those three capabilities has a direct read-across to the SAP data landscape.

Integration is where entity resolution lives, continuous matching across connected systems, merging fragmented records into the golden records AI can actually trust. SAP Business Data Cloud operationalizes this for the SAP ecosystem, harmonizing data objects across S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, and the broader application landscape into governed, consistent business entities.

Cataloguing is where most organizations are further behind than they think. A static data catalog is a directory, useful for humans, insufficient for AI. What the framework calls for is active cataloguing: machine learning continuously profiling data as it arrives, tracking lineage, building a semantic map that AI agents can traverse to understand not just what data exists, but how everything relates.

Governance is the capability that scales worst without intentional architecture. Traditional governance was built for humans who read policies and exercise judgment. AI agents don't do that. The framework's answer is policy-as-code: access rules enforced at the infrastructure level, before the model ever sees the data.

---

## The timing

The framework publishes this month. On June 25th, the ODI team (Elena Simperl, Paul Groth, and Stuart Coleman) will present it live and take questions. One hour, free.

[Register for the June 25th webinar]

And if you want early access to the full report before it's publicly indexed:

[Join IDEA]

---

The organizations that figure out the connection problem are the ones that will scale AI reliably. Everyone else will keep running pilots.

Where does your organization sit? Have you started the architectural conversation, or is it still downstream of the model selection debate?

---

IDEA — the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI — is a programme of independent research and peer learning developed in partnership between SAP and the Open Data Institute. Nine roundtables. 140+ senior executives. Three research briefs. And a framework that changes how enterprise AI readiness gets measured.

entry.title: Working with Large Data Models in SAP Datasphere Using Claude Code
entry.content:
Where We Left Off

A few weeks ago, my colleague @Yonggang_Yu published a great blog post: Using Claude Code and Natural Language to Create SAP Datasphere Artifacts

I loved the idea: describe what you need in plain language, and Claude Code translates that into the right CLI commands to create tables, views, and analytic models in Datasphere. I forked his repo and started playing around with it.

It worked really well. But after a while, I ran into a limitation.

The Problem: You Rarely Start from Scratch

When I started testing, I was creating fresh tables and views. That's fun, but it's not what most real projects look like.

In practice, you're usually working inside an existing data model. Maybe it's been growing for months. There are 50 tables, 120 views, 3 analytic models and they all depend on each other in ways that aren't always obvious. A business requirement comes in: "we need to add a country code to the reporting layer." Or a data architect says a column needs to be renamed to match a new naming standard.

That's when the original skills hit a wall. They're great at creating new objects, but they don't help you understand what you already have, or how a change to one object ripples through everything downstream.

I wanted to solve that problem. I used the SAP Group Reporting data model in Datasphere as my test case as it's a real-world, complex model with a deep chain of views layered on top of consolidated finance tables. Exactly the kind of thing you'd find in a production environment. And I happened to have worked on it recently and knew it fairly well. Also, this data model is available to anyone who wants to try for themselves.

The Challenge: Dependency Chains and Circular Deadlocks

To understand why this is hard, you need to know a bit about how Datasphere enforces consistency.

When you have a chain like this:

SAP_FI_SOURCE_TABLE
    └── SAP_FI_INTERMEDIATE_VIEW
            └── SAP_FI_REPORTING_VIEW
                    └── AM_FINANCE

...and you want to add a new column, you can't just add it to the source table and call it done. Every view in the chain needs to be updated to pass that column through. With 4 objects that's annoying. With 20 objects it's a real problem and takes a lot of time in the Datasphere UI.

Renaming or removing a column is even harder. Datasphere enforces referential integrity on every save. If you try to rename a column on a view, DSP blocks it with an HTTP 422 error, because downstream objects still reference the old name. But if you try to update the downstream object first, DSP blocks that too - because the upstream view doesn't have the new name yet.

The solution is a flag called --allow-missing-dependencies. It tells Datasphere: "save this, even if some references don't resolve yet, they'll be fixed shortly." With this flag, the deadlock breaks. Order stops mattering. You can update all objects freely and Datasphere sorts it out once everything is saved.

Figuring this out and baking it into every skill was one of the key things I had to get right.

The New Skills

I added five new skills on top of Yonggang's original six. Together they cover the "working inside an existing model" use case.

impact-analysis - understand before you change

Before touching anything, you (and Claude!) want to know what you're dealing with. This skill scans the entire space, builds a dependency graph of every view and analytic model, and shows you the full upstream/downstream chain for any object.

You tell Claude Code:

"What objects depend on SAP_FI_IL_A_ConsolidationUnit? Show me everything downstream."

Claude runs the impact-analysis skill and you get a tree like this in the terminal:

SAP_FI_IL_A_ConsolidationUnit [view]
  └── SAP_FI_ConsolidationUnit [view]          (direct)
        └── SAP_FI_IL_T_ConsolidationUnit [view]  (direct)
              └── AM_ConsolidationUnit [AM]         (direct)

Every object is tagged by type ([table], [view], [AM]), ordered from closest to furthest. You can also ask "which of these views are missing column COUNTRY_CODE?" and the skill highlights exactly which ones need updating, giving you a ready-made action plan before a single change is made.

The first run reads the whole space (~2-3 minutes for 100+ objects). After that, results are cached and every subsequent query is instant.

propagate-columns - add a column end-to-end

Once you've added a column to a source table, this skill cascades it through every downstream view automatically. You tell Claude:

"Add COUNTRY_CODE (3-char string) to SAP_FI_IL_A_ConsolidationUnit and make sure it appears in all downstream views."

Claude seeds the table, then runs the propagation. The terminal shows progress as each view is updated and verified:

[1/4] SAP_FI_ConsolidationUnit          → updated ✓
[2/4] SAP_FI_IL_T_ConsolidationUnit     → updated ✓
[3/4] SAP_FI_IL_A_ConsolidationUnitHierDir → updated ✓
[4/4] AM_ConsolidationUnit              → updated ✓

All objects verified. Deploying in topological order...
Deploy complete.

It walks the graph in topological order (sources before consumers), updates each view, verifies the column is present, then deploys everything in sequence. You can also ask Claude to do a dry run first and it will print the full plan without touching anything.

rename-column-cascade - rename across the whole chain

You tell Claude:

"Rename CNSLDTN_UNIT to CONSOLIDATION_UNIT in SAP_FI_REPORTING_VIEW and all objects that depend on it."

Claude renames the column in the starting view, then propagates the change through every downstream view and analytic model. The --allow-missing-dependencies flag is used on every save automatically, so the circular deadlock described above never becomes your problem.

remove-column-cascade - remove across the whole chain

Same pattern as rename, but for removing a column. Useful when cleaning up deprecated fields that have spread across many views.

remove-column-cascade - remove across the whole chain

Same pattern as rename, but for removing a column. Useful when cleaning up deprecated fields that have spread across many views. Just tell Claude which column to remove and where to start and it handles the rest.

add-columns-to-table and add-columns-to-view

The building blocks used by the cascade skills above. These update a single object precisely - all three locations that Datasphere requires for graphical views (element definitions, SELECT projection, and the visual UI model). Getting any of these out of sync causes Datasphere to silently convert your graphical view to SQL mode, which is hard to recover from.

A Concrete Example: Group Reporting

Let me walk through a realistic scenario using the Group Reporting Data Model which is available as SAP Content.

The model has a deep chain: source integration tables feed intermediate views, which feed reporting views, which feed analytic models. A typical request: "The CDS Views in the connected S4 System have new columns, these need to be replicated to DSP and propagated all the way up to the analytic model."

Step 1: understand what's downstream

"Show me everything downstream of SAP_FIN_CS_IL_I_CNSLDTNGROUPSTRUCTURE_2, and check which objects are missing the column SEGMENT_CODE."

Claude runs impact-analysis and prints the dependency tree with a gap report at the bottom:

SAP_FIN_CS_IL_I_CNSLDTNGROUPSTRUCTURE_2 [table]
  └── SAP_FIN_CS_IL_A_ConsolidationUnit [view]      SEGMENT_CODE: missing ✗
        └── SAP_FIN_CS_ConsGroup [view]              SEGMENT_CODE: missing ✗
              └── SAP_FIN_CS_IL_T_ConsGroup [view]   SEGMENT_CODE: missing ✗
                    └── AM_GroupReporting [AM]        SEGMENT_CODE: missing ✗

Action plan: 4 objects need updating.

Step 2: make the change

"Add SEGMENT_CODE (string, 10 chars, label 'Segment Code') to that table and propagate it to all downstream views."

Claude seeds the source table, then cascades the column through the chain, updating, verifying, and deploying each object in order:

Adding SEGMENT_CODE to SAP_FIN_CS_IL_I_CNSLDTNGROUPSTRUCTURE_2... ✓

Propagating downstream:

Deploy complete.

What would have been an hour of manual UI work (opening each view, adding the column in three places, saving, deploying) runs unattended in a couple of minutes.

Limitations

This is still early stage work, and it's worth being honest about where it falls short.

The skills cover the most common operations well, but Claude doesn't always take the direct path. Sometimes it runs into loops, asks for permissions repeatedly, or tries to figure things out step by step when the skill should have handled it cleanly. This is partly a prompting problem, partly a maturity problem - the more you use it, the better you get at describing what you want clearly.

More importantly: always check what Claude did. The cascade skills make real changes to real objects and deploy them. The backups are there for a reason. Before calling something done, open a few of the affected views in the Datasphere UI and verify the column is actually there, the view still renders in graphical mode, and nothing looks off. Automated doesn't mean infallible.

SQL views are a known gap - they can't be edited programmatically, so if one sits in the middle of a dependency chain, the cascade stops there and you have to finish manually. This could probably be solved by additional skills, but I have not looked into it yet.

What's Next

There's a lot of room to grow here. Better SQL view support, smarter conflict resolution, maybe skills that read a requirements doc and figure out what needs changing on their own, like Yonggang mentioned at the end of his Blog Post. I'm planning to keep adding things as I run into new use cases, and I'd genuinely welcome contributions from anyone who hits a gap and wants to fill it.

The code for all new skills is on GitHub: https://github.com/FredericWall/dsp-cli
Feel free to check the repo out, any contribution would be appreciated 🙂

Thanks to Yonggang for the original project and the idea. This is built directly on top of his work.
